coredata.dc:title: Enhancing thermoelectric properties of higher manganese silicide through facilitated non-equilibrium reactions via the introduction of additional Carbon nano-dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qijie
Wang, Qing
Zhang, Hailan
Xu, Longxiang
Zhao, Liedong
Wang, Jianglong
Cao, Qian
Ding, Zhihai
Wang, Shufang
Li, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: Higher manganese silicide (HMS) has gained significant attention as a compelling choice for thermoelectric (TE) applications at medium temperatures due to its abundance, environmental friendliness, and impact resistance properties. Recent achievement in HMS involves leveraging the synergistic effect of energy filtering, modulation doping, and boundary scattering to enhance the TE performance. However, excellent TE properties on HMS usually demands extreme preparation processes such as shock compression, melt spinning technique, or chemical vapor transport approach, as well as the use of rare and expensive elements or nano-dots (NDs), such as Re, Ge, Ag/Pt alloy, or CsPbBr3 quantum dots. In this study, a facile wet ball milling combing with spark plasma sintering is used to streamline the synthesis processes. Relatively green, cost-effective, and accessible C NDs are used to facilitated non-equilibrium reactions and enhance TE properties of HMS. Due to the C-doped effect, the PF increased from 1.42 to 1.62 mW m−1 K−2 at 723 K, representing an approximately 14 % improvement compared to the pristine HMS. The introduction of C, derived SiC and Mn NDs result in the enhancing phonon scattering, and leading to a reduction in lattice thermal conductivity from 2.21 to 1.81 W m−1 K−1. Consequently, the figure of merit (zT) increases from 0.42 to 0.55 at 823 K, representing an enhancement of approximately ∼ 31 %. Predictably, the incorporation of C NDs into semiconductors stands as one of the effective strategies for enhancing the TE properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermoelectric performance
Carbon nano-dot
Higher manganese silicide
Figure of merit
Peritectic reaction

This project is supported by State Key Laboratory of New Ceramic and Fine Processing Tsinghua University (No. KF202203). Funded by Science Research Project of Hebei Education Department (Grant No. JZX2024008). Hebei Province Optoelectronic Information Materials Laboratory Performance Subsidy Fund Project (No.22567634H).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Microwave-assisted synthesis experimental diagram. (b–e) Overall, enlarged morphologies, size distribution, and XRD pattern of the as-synthesized C NDs. (f–h) XRD patterns, enlarged figure, lattice parameters of HMS/C NDs composites.

The sample of HMS + 0.5 % C NDs: (a) Overall morphology image, (b) Grain boundary of HMS/C NDs composites, (c) HRTEM image and SAED pattern, (d) TEM image, (e) Enlarged perspective of the region enclosed by the box in Fig. 2(d), (f) dislocation diagram, (g–i) Strain maps of Fig. 2(f) along ε xx, ε xy, and ε yy directions.
(a–d) Overall morphology image and C, Mn, Si EDS mapppings of HMS/C NDs composites, (e,f) electron localization function diagrams.
XPS spectrum of (a–d) pure HMS, (e–h) HMS + 2.0 % C NDs composite sample.

(a) Temperature dependence of electrical conductivities of HMS + x % C NDs (x = 0.25, 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0) samples. (b, c) Calculated band structures of pristine HMS and C-doped HMS. (d, e) DOS and Projected density of states (PDOS) of pristine HMS and C-doped HMS. (f) Difference charge density of pristine HMS and C-doped HMS with iso-surface level of 0.012. (g) Work functions and supercell structures with vacuum layer of pristine HMS, Mn, C, and SiC. Temperature dependence of electrical properties of HMS + x % C NDs (x = 0.25, 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0) samples. (h) Seebeck coefficients, (i) Power factors.

(a) κ tot, (b) L and κ e, (c) κ
l , and (d) zT values of pristine HMS and HMS/C NDs composites as a function of temperature. (e) Crystal structure simulation diagram and (f) Schematic of reducing κ
l by multiscale phonon-scattering centers.

head.ce:title: Enhancing thermoelectric properties of higher manganese silicide through facilitated non-equilibrium reactions via the introduction of additional Carbon nano-dots

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qijie Zhang and Qing Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Higher manganese silicide (HMS) has gained significant attention as a compelling choice for thermoelectric (TE) applications at medium temperatures due to its abundance, environmental friendliness, and impact resistance properties. Recent achievement in HMS involves leveraging the synergistic effect of energy filtering, modulation doping, and boundary scattering to enhance the TE performance. However, excellent TE properties on HMS usually demands extreme preparation processes such as shock compression, melt spinning technique, or chemical vapor transport approach, as well as the use of rare and expensive elements or nano-dots (NDs), such as Re, Ge, Ag/Pt alloy, or CsPbBr3 quantum dots. In this study, a facile wet ball milling combing with spark plasma sintering is used to streamline the synthesis processes. Relatively green, cost-effective, and accessible C NDs are used to facilitated non-equilibrium reactions and enhance TE properties of HMS. Due to the C-doped effect, the PF increased from 1.42 to 1.62 mW m−1 K−2 at 723 K, representing an approximately 14 % improvement compared to the pristine HMS. The introduction of C, derived SiC and Mn NDs result in the enhancing phonon scattering, and leading to a reduction in lattice thermal conductivity from 2.21 to 1.81 W m−1 K−1. Consequently, the figure of merit (zT) increases from 0.42 to 0.55 at 823 K, representing an enhancement of approximately ∼ 31 %. Predictably, the incorporation of C NDs into semiconductors stands as one of the effective strategies for enhancing the TE properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Currently, exploring alternative energy sources has become a significant concern for nations due to the increasing global energy demand, given the non-renewable nature of fossil fuels and their detrimental environmental impact [1]. As a green, environmentally friendly technology capable of directly converting thermal energy into electrical energy, TE technology has the potential to convert various energy sources, including solar energy, household heating, automobile exhaust, and industrial waste heat, into electricity [2]. Its simple structure, high reliability, absence of noise, and lack of pollution make TE technology a promising alternative for addressing the challenges posed by the energy crisis. To assess the quality of a TE material, the dimensionless figure of merit,
zT =
S
2
σ T /
κ
tot
, serves as a suitable parameter for characterizing TE performance, where S, σ, T, κ tot are Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, absolute temperature and total thermal conductivity, respectively [3–5]. At present, the majority of outstanding TE materials, including those based on Bi2Te3 [6–8], PbTe [9,10], GeTe [11–13], SnSe [14–16], and Cu2Se [17–20], still face significant challenges in scaling up for practical applications due to inherent drawbacks. These limitations include the presence of toxic elements, insufficient thermal and chemical stability, as well as complex preparation processes and high manufacturing costs.

HMSs have garnered significant attention owing to their abundant elemental reserves, non-toxic nature, and outstanding mechanical property [21–23]. Characterized by a relatively stable Nowotny chimney-ladder (NCL) structure, which encompasses phases such as Mn4Si7, Mn7Si12, Mn11Si19, Mn15Si26, Mn19Si33, Mn27Si47, and Mn39Si68 [24–26]. TE materials based on HMS exhibit favorable electrical properties. HMSs exhibit a distinctive NCL phase, composed of two tetragonal subsystems denoted as [Mn] and [Si], each with incongruent c-axis. The silicon-rich NCL phase is commonly designated as MnSi γ , where γ represents the ratio of the c-axis lengths of the [Mn] and [Si] subsystems, expressed as γ = c Mn/c Si. Its value typically falls within the range of 1.72–1.75. Within the theoretical framework of (3 + 1)-dimensional superspace methods, it becomes feasible to accurately evaluate structural parameters modulated by inherent incommensurability. Consequently, by judiciously selecting appropriate dopants, favorable modulation of γ can be achieved [25,27–30]. High power factor (PF) of ∼ 1.5 × 10−3 W m−1 K−2 at 773 K were attained in the pristine HMS[31,32], which even surpassing that of SnSe (∼ 1.0 × 10−3 W m−1 K−2) [15,16]. Nonetheless, the κ tot in pristine HMS is relatively high (2.76 W m−1 K−1 at 823 K), leading to a low zT, which hovers around ∼ 0.40 ± 0.05. Hence, to optimize the TE performance, it is imperative to concentrate on reducing κ tot [33], while concurrently ensuring that electrical performance remains unaffected or potentially enhanced. In recent years, the majority of research findings have indicated that phonon scattering is augmented through modulation doping [34–37], grain refinement [38–40], composite nanostructures [41–44], and the introduction of mass or stress fluctuations [45–47] as strategies to mitigate κ tot. Chauhan et al. [34] achieved carrier concentration optimization, enhanced phonon scattering within the crystal, and lowered lattice thermal conductivity by doping Al in HMS. This resulted in a maximum zT value of 0.75 at 873 K. Prajapati et al. [40] synthesized a p-Type HMS-CrSi2 composite using the MS-SPS method. This synthesis process involves rapid solidification melt spinning, resulting in the formation of nano-sized grains and yielding a zT value of 0.92. Kuryliuk et al. [48] incorporated Ge NDs on SiO2 layers to enhance phonon scattering, consequently reducing κ tot. MnS nano-particles [43], MnTe nanowires [49], Ag/Pt alloy QDs [50] were incorporated into HMS bulks to reduce κ
l to ∼ 2.65–1.92 W m−1K−1 by increasing grain boundaries, dislocations, and point defects, which resulting in a zT max of 0.5–0.7 at 823 K. Incorporating C ND [48,51–53] or nanotube [17], graphene [54], C60 [55] and other carbon-based nanostructures into TE materials is also an effective approach to significantly enhance the TE properties. For instance, Hu et al. [17] embed C NDs into Cu2Se, resulting in the achievement of a zT of ∼ 1.98 at 973 K because of the enhancing energy filtering and phonon scattering effect. Feng et al. [54] introduced graphene into CoSb3 matrix, led to a notable enhancement of σ and reduction of κ
l . Consequently, incorporating C nanostructures into TE materials has a substantial impact on enhancing TE properties.

Carbon NDs is the quasi-zero-dimensional nanostructure with diameters of ∼ 10 nm, which can exert a relatively significant phonon scattering effect within the matrix with low phonon mean free path [56–59]. Especially for HMS-based materials containing Si elements of the same family, excess C may interfere with the peritectic reaction of MnSi and Si, leading to the destruction of the long-range ordered structure of the HMS and resulting in a reduction in κ
l . In this study, we employed wet ball milling in conjunction with spark plasma sintering (SPS) to incorporate additional C NDs into HMSs, achieving an energy filtering effect and multi-scale phonon scattering to enhance the PF of HMSs while reducing the κ
l . At 823 K, the zT max increased to 0.55, representing an approximately 31% improvement compared to the pristine HMS.

HMS bulks were prepared using a facile, short-time, and low-energy strategy, wet ball milling combined with SPS. High-purity raw powders of Mn (99.95 %, 200 mmol), Si (99.99 %, 358 mmol), and n-hexane (50 mL) were mixed via wet ball milling for 2.0 h at the speed of 350 rpm [33,50,53]. In the mixing process, three kinds of tungsten carbide balls with the diameter of 3, 6, 10 mm were used, whose number are 400, 120, 20, respectively. The powder ratio is ∼ 20:1. The C NDs were prepared by the microwave-assisted synthesis method [60,61]. 58.1 mmol trisodium citrate dehydrate (C6H5O7Na3) and 333 mmol urea (H2NCONH2) were dissolved in 100 mL dimethylformamide (DMF). Then, the 20 mL solution was put into a microwave oven and heated at 500 W for 10 min. After that, a part of the aqueous solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to separate large carbon impurity particles. Finally, the supernatant was collected, and the solution exhibited a uniform brown and black color, indicating the generation of C ND. Next, 20 mL of C NDs/n-hexane solution was added to the tungsten carbide jar, followed by continued ball milling for 30 min. The mixture was adequately dried to powders in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 6.0 h. The dried powders were sintered at 1050 °C for 6.0 min under the axial pressure of 60 MPa in SPS (SPFDS-10T, China). The disks of 1.5 mm thickness and rectangular columns with 12 mm × 2.5 mm × 2.5 mm were sliced along the directions perpendicular and parallel to the axial pressure, respectively, to evaluate the thermal and electrical transport properties along the axial pressure direction.

The crystal phases and microstructures of samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) system (Nova Nano SEM450, FEI, USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) system (ESCALAB 250Xi, UK), and Aberration-Corrected Transmission Electron Microscopy (JEM-ARM200F, JEOL, Japan). The temperature-dependent σ and S were evaluated using the LSR3–1000 Seebeck coefficient/electrical resistance measuring system. Hall coefficients, carrier concentrations, and mobilities were measured using a Hall measurement system based on the Van der Pauw method (ET9107-HS, East Changing Technologies, Inc.). The κ tot was calculated using κ tot = DC p ρ, where D, C p, and ρ are the thermal diffusion coefficient, specific heat capacity, and density, respectively. D (Fig. S2(a)) was evaluated using a laser flash device (LFA 1000, LINSEIS, Germany). C p was calculated in according to accordance with the Dulong–Petit Law, and ρ was measured using Archimedes' Archimedes’ principle (Fig. S2(b)).

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the Vienna ab initio Simulation Package based on the projector-augmented wave method [62,63]. The exchange and correlation functional was treated by the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation [64]. Tetragonal Mn32Si56 and Mn32Si55C supercells were employed to investigate the changes in the band structures of MnSi1.79 and HMS/C NDs sample, respectively. The structure was fully optimized until the Hellmann-Feynman force on each atom was less than 0.01 eV Å−1. The plane-wave function basis was used to expand the wave functions, and the energy cutoff was 500 eV. A 3 × 6 × 2 Monkhorst-Pack k-mesh was generated for self-consistent calculations.

C NDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted synthesis method ( Fig. 1(a)), and corresponding morphology, size distribution, and XRD pattern is presented in Fig. 1(b–d). TEM images (Fig. 1(b, c)) reveal that the as-synthesized C NDs have a uniform distribution with the size of ∼ 10 nm (Fig. 1(d)). A characteristic peak corresponding to the C (002) crystal plane is observed at 2θ = 25.1° (Fig. 1(e)), indicating the as-synthesized C NDs belong to the hexagonal structure (JCPDS card no. 41-1487).

Then C NDs were integrated into HMSs via a wet ball milling process followed by SPS, yielding HMS + x % C NDs (x = 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0) samples. The XRD pattern of the samples is illustrated in Fig. 1(f), and the diffraction peaks of the samples closely correspond to Mn15Si26 (JCPDS card no. 89-2413), suggesting that the prepared compounds primarily consist of the Mn15Si26 phase. With the inclusion of C NDs, the presence of SiC (0111) plane (2θ = 43.6°) and Mn (002) plane (2θ = 50.7°) was detected, suggesting an additional chemical reaction between Si powder and C NDs took place within the sample, resulting in the formation of SiC, along with a slight amount of Mn precipitation. An enlarged XRD pattern of the (2115) plane is presented in Fig. 1(g). With the increasing content of C NDs, the 2θ angle gradually shifts from 42.1° to 42.5°, leading to a progressive rightward shift of the XRD peak, and indicates lattice parameters a and c are reduced from 5.49, 65.32 Å to 5.44, 65.11 Å (Fig. 1(h)), respectively. As expected, the slight reduction in the lattice parameter is derived from the relatively short C-Si covalent bond comparing with Mn-Si bond (inset of Fig. 1(h)).

TEM images ( Fig. 2(a–f)) validate the distribution and size of C NDs within the composite sample, which includes 0.5 % C NDs and underwent SPS. Fig. 2(a) provides an overview of the morphology, illustrating a relatively broad particle size distribution of HMS with the average size of approximately 1.08 ± 0.39 μm (inset of Fig. 2(b)). Lots of grain boundaries, pores, and precipitates are observed in the HMS composites with 0.5 % C NDs. Fig. 2(b) reveals the presence of distinct grain boundaries in the composite sample. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image depicted in Fig. 2(c) exhibits parallel lattice fringes along the [1–20] zone axis, complemented by the associated SAED pattern (inset of Fig. 2(c)), revealing the lattice fringe spacing of (2115) plane, ∼ 2.153 Å. In the HMS/C NDs composite sample, a notable quantity of nano-inclusions with diameters ranging from approximately 3.0 to 12 nm was observed in the TEM images (Fig. 2(d)). Fig. 2(e) offers an enlarged perspective of the region enclosed by the box in Fig. 2(d), with the inset presenting a Fourier-filtered image of the (4111) atomic plane. This reveals a dense cluster of dislocations in close proximity to the interface (highlighted with red T symbols). We utilized geometric phase analysis (GPA) [65] to compute strain maps in various directions based on the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) image in Fig. 2(f), which are illustrated in Fig. 2(g–i). These maps unveil fluctuations in the lattice strain field within the matrix. The presence of high-density dislocations, in conjunction with localized strains and phase interfaces linked to interfacial defect regions, can function as efficient scattering centers for phonons, consequently leading to a substantial reduction in κ
l .

In order to ascertain the elemental composition of the composite sample, energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) analysis was conducted, as illustrated in Fig. 3(a–d). The results of the EDS analysis unambiguously reveal the presence of prominent precipitates within the composite sample 1.0 % C NDs, notably including SiC and Mn nano-inclusions. When C NDs are introduced into HMS, non-equilibrium reactions occur during the SPS process, leading to preferential interactions between C and Si. This results in the formation of robust Si-C bonds, as clearly depicted in the electron localization function diagram (Fig. 3(e, f)), demonstrating a pronounced bonding interaction between C and Si.

The interaction between C and Si disrupts the initial crystallographic reaction equilibrium, leading to an excess precipitation of cationic Mn, ultimately resulting in the formation of Mn and SiC nano-inclusions. The underlying mechanism is elucidated through the chemical Eqs. (1)–(3) as depicted:
(1) Mn + Si → MnSi
(2) 15MnSi + 11Si + xC → Mn15Si26C x
(3) 26Mn15Si26C x → (26-x)Mn15Si26 + 26xSiC + 15xMn

In addition to XRD detection, for enhanced precision in analyzing the phase fractions of HMS, SiC, and Mn within the prepared samples, XPS analysis was performed on both the pure phase HMS and HMS + 2.0 % C NDs samples, as illustrated in Fig. 4(a–h). The results unveiled that the pure phase HMS retained Mn, Si, and MnSi phases as outlined in Eq. (1), with their respective energy values recorded as 639.10 eV, 99.70 eV, and 642.20 eV. As the concentration of C NDs increases, the presence of Mn4+ derived from MnSi decreases, accompanied by the emergence of the C4− peak (282.60 eV), indicating the formation of SiC and suggesting the progression of Eqs. (2) and (3). Throughout the entire non-equilibrium reaction process, the proportions of SiC and Mn gradually increase to 1.50 % and 3.26 %, respectively, in line with the XRD findings. It can be anticipated that these chemical bond substitutions and redistributions of elemental proportions will lead to modifications in the electrical properties and band structure of the composite.

The electrical transport properties of both pristine HMS and HMS/C NDs composite materials are illustrated in Fig. 5. Across the entire temperature range spanning from 323 to 823 K, σ of HMS/C NDs composites consistently exceeds that of the pristine HMS sample, as depicted in Fig. 5(a). At 823 K, with the gradual increase in C NDs content up to 2.0 %, σ increases from 27 × 103 to 34 × 103 S/m. This is primarily attributed to the C atoms occupying the Si sites, which contribute to a higher n. In the theoretical band structure, the band gap decreases from the initial 0.82 eV (Mn32Si56, Fig. 5(b)) to 0.75 eV (Mn32Si55C, Fig. 5(c)). The corresponding density of states (DOS) plot reveals that C 2p orbital electrons introduces impurity energy levels near the conduction band (Fig. 5(d, e)), resulting in a reduced band gap and an increased concentration of thermally excited charge carriers. The difference charge density map (Fig. 5(f)) further illustrates that greater electronegativity of C results in the shared electrons in C-Mn bonds having a stronger tendency to localize on the C atom, particularly in comparison to Mn-Si bonds. The bonding between C and Si is favored over the bonding between Si and Si, promoting the adjustment of energy band and the precipitation of SiC. Furthermore, with the inclusion of C NDs, nanoscale particles of Mn, C NDs, and SiC precipitate within the HMS matrix. To delve deeper into the changes in electrical properties, we conducted DFT simulations to qualitatively analyze the theoretical work functions of pristine HMS (4.34 eV), Mn (4.47 eV), C (4.76 eV) and SiC (7.81 eV). These simulations were conducted using supercell structures with the vacuum thickness of 15 Å, as illustrated in Fig. 5(g). The Fermi level of Mn, C, and SiC are sequentially lower than that of the HMS matrix. Consequently, the holes originating from Mn, C NDs, and SiC are transferred to the HMS matrix, resulting in a gradual increase of n. Moreover, the phenomenon of charge transfer can also result in a slight increase in the n of the material.

The curves of S demonstrate non-linear trends with increasing temperature, indicative of the characteristic behavior of degenerate semiconductors. The proportional relationship among σ, S, and n accorded well with the classical equations of electrical transport in a degenerate semiconductor:
(4)
S =
8
π
2
k
B
2
3 e
h
2
m
*
T
(
π
3 n
)
2 / 3
where n, μ , k
B , h, and m* are the carrier concentration, mobility, Boltzmann constant, Planck constant, and density-of-state effective mass, respectively. In contrast to σ, as depicted in Fig. 5(h), S values of the composite steadily declines throughout the entire temperature range as the C NDs content increases. This phenomenon is mainly attributed to the elevated n, which leads to a decrease in S, as indicated by Eq. (4). The deceleration observed in the reduction magnitude of the S coefficient may be attributed to the barrier existing at the HMS/SiC interface. The potential barrier filtering effect is elaborated upon in Fig. S3 of the Supplementary Information (SI). In addition, based on the flattening valence band maximum (inset of Fig. 5(h), we calculated the effective mass (m*) values for the holes, it is observed that the m* of the C-doped HMS sample increases from 4.5
m
e
*
(pristine HMS) to 6.6
m
e
*
. The gradually increasing m* to some extent mitigates the decrease of S, and correspondingly enhances the PF. As C NDs are introduced, the PF of the HMS/C NDs composite consistently rises, as illustrated in Fig. 5(i). At 723 K, in the HMS + 1.0 % C NDs sample, the PF max reaches ∼ 1.62 mW m−1 K−2, representing an ∼ 14 % improvement in comparison to the pristine HMS (∼ 1.42 mW m−1 K−2). Therefore, the incorporation of C NDs can effectively modulate the electrical properties of HMS through elemental doping and carrier concentration optimization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal transport properties and dimensionless figure of merit, ZT
ce:section.ce:para:
As illustrated in Fig. 6(a), κ tot of the HMS/C NDs composite samples is significantly lower than that of the pristine HMS sample. However, with the increasing C NDs content, κ tot does not exhibit a further noticeable change. The lowest κ tot is achieved at 673 K in the HMS + 2.0 % C NDs sample, measuring ∼ 2.24 W m−1 K−1. Normally, the κ tot typically comprises two main components: κ e (Fig. 6(b)) and κ
l (Fig. 6(c)). It can be estimated using the Wiedemann-Franz law，κ e = σLT，and κ
l = κ tot − κ e. Here, L represents the Lorenz constant, which can be estimated by an empirical formula, L ≈ 1.5 + exp(
−
|S|
116
) for degenerate semiconductor [24].

As the temperature increases, κ
l of each composite sample decreases proportionally, resulting in a significant decrease in κ tot. At 673 K, the HMS + 2.0 % C NDs sample shows the lowest κ
l , measuring ∼ 1.81 W m−1 K−1, representing an 18 % reduction compared to the pristine HMS. This observation can primarily be attributed to the introduction of multiscale phonon scattering centers within HMS (Fig. 6(e, f)), ranging from mesoscale (grain boundaries, nanoscale precipitates) to atomic scale (point defects or dislocations). Ultimately, at 823 K, the C NDs composite sample with 2.0 % C NDs achieves a maximum zT of 0.55 (Fig. 6(d)), signifying an enhancement of approximately 31 % in comparison to the pristine HMS. Fig. S3 depicts the κ
l and zT values of the current work in comparison with those of other materials [34,36,37,53,66,67]. It is evident that the HMS composite with C NDs exhibits relatively low lattice thermal conductivity and high zT value. The integration of C NDs proves to be of significance in the quest to optimize TE performance.

The non-equilibrium reactions that occur following the incorporation C NDs into the HMS bulk effectively enhance the bonding between C and Si, leading to the partial precipitation of Mn and the formation of SiC nano-inclusions. This multifaceted effect represents one of the feasible strategies for simultaneously optimizing thermoelectric performance. As a result of the increased carrier concentration, the PF of the HMS + 1.0 % C NDs sample demonstrates an improvement of approximately 14 % compared to the pristine HMS, reaching ∼ 1.62 mW m−1 K−2 at 723 K. The charge transfer facilitated by nanoscale precipitates formed after the incorporation of C NDs optimizes the carrier concentration. Compared to the pristine HMS sample, the κ
l of the HMS + 2.0 % C NDs sample reduced by ∼ 18 % (∼ 1.81 W m−1 K−1 at 673 K). This reduction is attributed to the synergistic effects of adding C NDs and the enhancement of mid-wave and short-wave phonon scattering due to the presence of Mn and SiC nanoparticles. At 823 K, the C NDs composite sample achieves a maximum zT of 0.55, indicating an approximate 31 % improvement compared to the pristine HMS. The findings suggest that incorporation of NDs to achieve element doping, charge transfer effects and multiscale phonon scattering represents an effective strategy for enhancing TE performance.

Zhiliang Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shufang Wang: Visualization, Funding acquisition. Zhihai Ding: Resources. Qian Cao: Resources. Hailan Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Qing Wang: Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qijie Zhang: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jianglong Wang: Conceptualization. Liedong Zhao: Data curation. Longxiang Xu: Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hebei Provincial Department of Science and Technology (236Z4403G), Research Innovation Team Project of Hebei University (IT2023A04, 150000321008). This project is supported by State Key Laboratory of New Ceramic and Fine Processing Tsinghua University (No. KF202203). Funded by Science Research Project of Hebei Education Department (Grant no. JZX2024008). Hebei Province Optoelectronic Information Materials Laboratory Performance Subsidy Fund Project (No. 22567634H).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.174963.
